facts_disclosed: Dev moved out, studio in St Pauls, £625/month

turns.content: alright so random question. whats the best way to make a small flat feel less depressing when you first move in. like on a budget obviously

Good question. A few cheap things that make a big difference in a small space:

- **Lighting** -- avoid using just the main ceiling light. A warm-toned lamp or two makes a flat feel completely different. You can get decent ones from IKEA or a charity shop for under a tenner.
- **Something on the walls** -- even a few posters or photos. Bare walls make anywhere feel temporary.
- **Plants** -- a pothos or spider plant costs a few quid and makes a room feel alive.
- **Textiles** -- a throw on the sofa or bed, a rug if you've got hard floors.

What's the situation -- have you just moved somewhere new?

yeah man just moved into a studio in St Pauls. its alright you know decent area close to everything but its just bare empty and quiet. like too quiet. had to move quick because my flatmate Dev moved back to Leicester so the old place wasnt an option anymore

Ah, that's a big adjustment -- going from living with someone to a solo studio, especially when it happened quickly and wasn't really planned. The quietness hits different when you're used to having someone around.

St Pauls is a good spot though. How's the flat itself -- is it a decent size or properly tiny?

its small but its fine for one person. kitchen area and living space in one room then a separate bedroom and bathroom. £625 a month which is more than i was paying before but its just me now so i cant split it you know. student budget taking a hit ngl

£625 for a studio in St Pauls isn't bad by Bristol standards, but yeah going from splitting rent to paying solo is a proper jump. That's a significant chunk of student finance.

Have you looked into whether you qualify for any council tax exemption? Full-time students are usually exempt from council tax, which would save you a fair bit on top of the rent.

yeah im exempt from council tax because of uni which is a lifesaver tbh. bills are included in the rent as well which helps. its just the rent itself thats more. like before i was paying 475 and now its 625 so thats 150 extra a month which adds up you know

That's £150 extra a month, so £1800 over the year -- definitely adds up. Bills included does help with predictability though, at least you know exactly what's going out each month.

Are you managing with your student loan and any other income, or is it going to be tight?

its going to be tight but manageable if i budget properly. i get a bit from coaching as well not loads but it covers food mostly. i just need to stop buying unnecessary stuff. like i bought new trainers last week that i definitely didnt need haha

Ha, we've all been there with the unnecessary trainer purchase. But seriously, if coaching covers food and your loan covers rent, you're in a workable position. Just need to be disciplined with the discretionary spending.

A simple budget tip: on the day your loan comes in, immediately transfer rent and any fixed costs to a separate account or pot. Whatever's left in your main account is your actual spending money. Keeps it honest.

thats actually smart yeah. i should set up a standing order for the rent straight away so its gone before i can touch it. tbh the money side is stressful but the hardest bit is just being on my own. like me and Dev werent best mates or anything but having someone in the house was nice. come back from placement and just chat about the day or watch something together. now its just me and the walls

That's completely normal and it's worth acknowledging rather than brushing past. The social side of having a flatmate is easy to undervalue until it's gone -- those small daily interactions add up to a lot of background connection.

A few things that might help with the adjustment:
- Keep your routine social where you can -- coaching nights, study sessions at the library instead of home, even calling family while you cook
- Don't fall into the trap of just staying in the studio all the time because it's easier
- Give it a few weeks. Once the flat feels like yours and not just some empty room you moved into, it'll feel different

Do you see people from your course regularly outside of placement?

yeah i see course mates at uni and on placement and i coach twice a week so its not like im isolated or anything. its just the evenings and weekends that feel weird. but youre right i should make more effort to study at the library or go to nans instead of sitting here. nans place is always warm and theres always food so thats the obvious choice haha

Nan's place sounds like the clear winner -- warmth, food, and company. Can't beat that. And St Pauls is probably not too far from her either?

Honestly, give yourself some grace with this transition. You went from a shared place to living alone at short notice. It takes a few weeks to settle into a new normal. Once you've got some stuff on the walls, a lamp or two, and a routine that gets you out of the flat regularly, it'll start feeling like home.

yeah nans not far which is a plus. and youre right i just need to make it mine. might get some photos printed of the football lads and some family pics for the wall. and a proper lamp like you said not just that bare ceiling light making everything look like a hospital. alright cheers man this has been helpful. going to go sort this place out a bit

Photos of the team and family on the walls will make a massive difference -- turns it from a random flat into your space. Get that lamp sorted and it'll feel like a different room.

Good luck settling in. St Pauls is a solid area and the studio will feel like home soon enough. Take care.
